questions.question: Define the following terms with examples: (a) Tuple (b) Attribute (c) Domain (d) Degree of a relation
keyPoints: Tuple: A row/record in a relation, e.g., in Student(SID,Name,Age), (101,'John',20) is a tuple
Attribute: A column/field representing a property, e.g., Name, Age in Student relation
Domain: Set of permissible values for an attribute, e.g., Age domain = {1,2,...,100}
Degree: Number of attributes in a relation, e.g., Student(SID,Name,Age,City) has degree 4

questions.question: Differentiate between Primary Key and Alternate Key with an example.
keyPoints: Primary Key: Selected candidate key used to uniquely identify tuples; must be NOT NULL
Alternate Key: Candidate key not chosen as Primary Key; also unique but not used as primary identifier
Example: In Student(SID,Email,Phone), if SID is PK, then Email and Phone are Alternate Keys

questions.question: What is a Foreign Key? Explain its purpose and the integrity rule associated with it.
keyPoints: Foreign Key: Attribute(s) in a relation that reference Primary Key of another relation
Purpose: Establishes relationship between tables, enables data integrity across tables
Referential Integrity: Foreign key value must either match a PK value in referenced table OR be NULL
Example: In Order(CustomerID, OrderID), CustomerID references Customer table's PK

questions.question: Explain Entity Integrity Rule and Referential Integrity Rule with examples.
keyPoints: Entity Integrity: Primary Key cannot contain NULL values; ensures each tuple is uniquely identifiable
Example: Student(SID,Name) - SID cannot be NULL
Referential Integrity: Foreign key must reference existing Primary Key or be NULL
Example: Order.CustomerID must match a valid Customer.SID or be NULL

questions.question: Given a relation R(A,B,C,D) with A as Primary Key, identify: (i) Super Keys (ii) Candidate Keys (iii) Alternate Keys
keyPoints: Super Keys: Any set containing A - {A}, {A,B}, {A,C}, {A,B,C}, {A,B,C,D}, etc.
Candidate Keys: Minimal keys - {A} is the only minimal key (since A alone is unique)
Alternate Keys: None in this case, because A is the only candidate key chosen as PK

questions.question: What is a Composite Key? Give an example where a composite key is necessary.
keyPoints: Composite Key: A candidate key consisting of two or more attributes
Necessary when no single attribute can uniquely identify tuples
Example: Enrolled(StudentID, CourseID, Semester, Grade) - neither StudentID nor CourseID alone can identify enrollment; combination of (StudentID, CourseID, Semester) is needed

questions.question: Explain the difference between Degree and Cardinality of a relation with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Degree: Number of attributes (columns) in a relation - static structure
Cardinality: Number of tuples (rows) in a relation - changes with data
Example: Employee(EmpID, Name, Dept, Salary) has degree 4; if 50 employees exist, cardinality is 50

questions.question: Consider a relation Student(SID, Name, Age, Email, Phone, City, CourseID) where SID is the primary key. (a) What are the possible candidate keys? (b) If CourseID references Course(CourseID, CourseName), what type of key is CourseID in Student relation? (c) Explain why Email or Phone could serve as Alternate Keys.
keyPoints: (a) Candidate Keys: {SID} (minimal, chosen as PK), also {Email} and {Phone} if unique for each student
(b) CourseID is a Foreign Key in Student relation referencing Course relation's Primary Key
(c) Email and Phone are Alternate Keys because they uniquely identify students but were not chosen as Primary Key; they satisfy candidate key properties (minimal and unique)
(d) Any set containing SID is a Super Key - emphasizing the hierarchical relationship

questions.question: Explain the relational database concepts of Super Key, Candidate Key, and Primary Key. How do they differ? Also, given relation R(A,B,C,D) where {A,B} is the primary key, identify: (i) Two super keys (ii) One candidate key that is not the primary key
keyPoints: Super Key: Set of attributes that uniquely identify tuples; may contain redundant attributes
Candidate Key: Minimal super key with no proper subset being a key; must be unique and not redundant
Primary Key: Selected candidate key used to identify tuples; must be unique and NOT NULL
Difference: Super Key is superset, Candidate Key is minimal, Primary Key is the chosen one
(i) Super Keys containing A,B: {A,B}, {A,B,C}, {A,B,D}, {A,B,C,D}
(ii) If C alone is unique, then {C} could be a candidate key; otherwise all attributes forming a unique set can be candidate keys

questions.question: Design a relational database schema for a College with entities: Student, Course, and Enrollment. Explain what Primary Keys and Foreign Keys you would use, and how referential integrity is maintained.
keyPoints: Student(SID PK, Name, Age, City)
Course(CourseID PK, CourseName, Credits)
Enrollment(EnrollmentID PK, SID FK->Student, CourseID FK->Course, Grade)
Primary Keys: Student.SID, Course.CourseID, Enrollment.EnrollmentID
Foreign Keys: Enrollment.SID references Student.SID; Enrollment.CourseID references Course.CourseID

Referential Integrity: Enrollment.SID must match existing Student.SID or be NULL; Enrollment.CourseID must match existing Course.CourseID or be NULL; ON DELETE CASCADE or SET NULL options can be defined

questions.question: Define NULL in databases and explain why it is important. Also discuss the challenges NULL values create in database operations.
keyPoints: NULL: Represents missing, unknown, or not applicable information; not same as zero or empty string
Importance: Essential for handling incomplete data; represents real-world scenarios where information is unavailable

Challenges: NULL + anything = NULL (arithmetic), NULL = NULL is FALSE (comparison), requires special IS NULL/IS NOT NULL operators, complicates aggregate functions (COUNT ignores NULLs differently), can cause unexpected results in queries
Example: Age+5 when Age is NULL gives NULL, not 5; WHERE Age=NULL returns no rows because NULL is not equal to anything including itself
